title: SEGMENT 11 — oxBS-seq — adding an oxidation step to distinguish 5mC from 5hmC

lines.text: SEGMENT 11 — oxBS-seq — adding an oxidation step to distinguish 5mC from 5hmC

The technique called oxidative bisulfite sequencing, or oxBS-seq, was published in 2012 by Shankar Balasubramanian's lab at Cambridge — yes, the same Balasubramanian who, with Klenerman, invented Illumina sequencing back in the late 1990s.
The Balasubramanian lab in the 2010s pivoted hard into the chemistry of modified cytosine bases, and oxBS-seq was one of their most influential contributions to the methylation field.
The chemistry is beautiful in its simplicity.
The problem with standard bisulfite is that both 5mC and 5hmC resist conversion to U, so you can't tell them apart.
The oxBS solution is to add a chemical pre-treatment, using potassium perruthenate (KRuO4), which selectively oxidizes 5hmC to 5-formylcytosine (5fC) without touching 5mC.
The crucial property of 5fC is that, unlike 5hmC, it does react with bisulfite and gets converted to U.

So after the oxidation step, the cytosines look like this: unmethylated C still converts to U; 5hmC has been oxidized to 5fC and now also converts to U; only 5mC survives intact and reads as C in the sequencing output.
So you run two parallel experiments on aliquots of the same sample.
Experiment A: standard bisulfite sequencing.
Both 5mC and 5hmC survive; the readout is "5mC plus 5hmC" at every position.
Experiment B: oxidative bisulfite sequencing.
Only 5mC survives; the readout is "5mC alone" at every position.
Subtract Experiment B from Experiment A at each cytosine, and you get the 5hmC signal at that position.
Two assays, one subtraction, full discrimination between the two marks at base resolution across the entire genome.
The oxBS workflow is otherwise identical to standard WGBS — fragment, ligate methylated adapters, oxidize, bisulfite-convert, amplify, sequence, align.
The only addition is the oxidation step before bisulfite, and that step takes about an hour of additional bench work.
The downstream pipeline is the same.

oxBS-seq became, for a few years in the mid-2010s, the standard method for studying 5hmC genomically — particularly in brain tissue, where 5hmC is abundant, and in early embryonic development, where 5hmC accumulates as part of the reprogramming process.
The Balasubramanian lab and others used oxBS to publish the first base-resolution 5hmC maps of human and mouse tissues, opening up the molecular biology of hydroxymethylation as a measurable mark.
But oxBS inherits the underlying limitations of bisulfite.
It still damages DNA — in fact, the additional oxidation step adds to the damage.
It still requires high input.
It still suffers from three-base alignment difficulties.
And because you have to run two parallel experiments, your sequencing cost roughly doubles per sample.
For applications where you need to discriminate 5mC and 5hmC and you have plenty of input, oxBS is a clean solution.
For low-input or damage-sensitive applications, it doesn't help with the fundamental problem.
There's a related technique called TAB-seq — TET-assisted bisulfite sequencing — that takes the opposite approach.

Where oxBS oxidizes 5hmC further so it converts with bisulfite, TAB-seq uses the TET enzymes to oxidize 5mC all the way to 5-carboxylcytosine (which converts) while 5hmC is first protected by chemical glucosylation.
After bisulfite, only 5hmC survives as a C in the readout — you get a direct 5hmC-specific map without needing a subtraction.

TAB-seq, published by Chuan He's lab at the University of Chicago in 2012, is the complementary tool to oxBS-seq: oxBS reads 5mC alone; TAB-seq reads 5hmC alone; combine either with standard bisulfite (which reads both) and you have a complete picture.
These oxidative and enzymatic bisulfite variants opened up the discrimination problem but didn't solve the damage problem.
For the damage problem, you needed to escape from bisulfite chemistry entirely.
The two techniques that did so — TAPS and EM-seq — emerged in 2019, almost simultaneously, and they are reshaping the methylation field.
